symptoms.description: Forceful ejection of stomach contents through the mouth, often preceded by retching or nausea.

possible_causes.brief: Ate spoiled food, garbage, grass, or rich/fatty food
Especially in puppies or unwormed dogs
Swallowed toy, bone, sock - can be life-threatening
Inflammation of the pancreas, often after fatty meal
Highly contagious, often fatal without treatment

home_care: Withhold food for 12 hours (water still allowed in small amounts)
Offer small amounts of water frequently to prevent dehydration
Reintroduce bland diet: plain boiled chicken with white rice in small portions
Gradually return to normal food over 2-3 days once vomiting stops
Avoid rich, fatty, or unfamiliar foods
Allow rest (no exercise) and skip walks until recovered

vet_urgency.low: Single vomit, dog seems otherwise well - monitor at home, contact vet if it persists beyond 24 hours
vet_urgency.medium: Multiple episodes in 24 hours with mild lethargy - phone vet for advice within 24 hours
vet_urgency.high: Continuous vomiting, blood in vomit, bloated belly, collapse, or puppy with vomiting - seek emergency vet care immediately

red_flags: Blood in vomit (red, brown, or coffee-ground appearance)
Continuous retching with little brought up (possible GDV/twisted stomach)
Unable to keep water down for more than 12 hours
Suspected ingestion of toxic substance or foreign object

combinations.concern: Possible gastroenteritis, pancreatitis, or parvovirus - vet visit within 24h
Possible GDV (gastric dilatation-volvulus) - EMERGENCY, immediate vet
Possible ulcer, foreign body, or serious illness - urgent vet care
Could indicate chronic illness - vet investigation needed

symptoms.description: Frequent, loose, or watery bowel movements, often more than usual in volume.

possible_causes.brief: New food, scavenging, or food intolerance

home_care: Withhold food for 12 hours (water always available)
Reintroduce with bland diet (boiled chicken + white rice, plain)
Small frequent meals rather than one large portion
Probiotic paste (vet-approved) can help restore gut flora
Gradually return to normal food over 3-4 days
Ensure fresh water is always available to prevent dehydration

vet_urgency.low: One or two soft stools, dog acting normal - monitor and offer bland diet
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent diarrhoea for more than 24-48 hours - phone vet for advice
vet_urgency.high: Bloody diarrhoea, severe dehydration, lethargy, or puppy with diarrhoea - emergency vet immediately

red_flags: Watery diarrhoea with foul smell (possible parvovirus)
Signs of dehydration (sunken eyes, dry gums, skin tenting)

combinations.concern: Risk of rapid dehydration - vet within 24h
Possible serious GI illness - vet visit recommended
Could indicate haemorrhagic gastroenteritis or parvovirus - urgent vet

symptoms.description: Reduced energy, unusual tiredness, less interest in normal activities like walks or play.

possible_causes.brief: Dogs hide pain by reducing activity
Reduced red blood cells - check gum colour
Always consider if onset is sudden

home_care: Allow rest in a quiet, comfortable space
Ensure fresh water is always available
Offer favourite gentle food to encourage appetite
Monitor temperature if possible (normal dog: 38.3-39.2°C / 101-102.5°F)
Check gums - should be pink and moist, not pale or yellow
Note when it started and any other subtle changes

vet_urgency.low: Mildly subdued after exercise or hot day, otherwise eating and drinking - monitor 24h
vet_urgency.medium: Lethargy lasting more than 24 hours with reduced appetite - phone vet
vet_urgency.high: Severe weakness, collapse, pale or yellow gums, difficulty breathing - emergency vet

red_flags: Pale, white, blue, or yellow gums
Combined with rapid or laboured breathing
Sudden onset after possible toxin exposure
Older dog with new severe lethargy

combinations.concern: Systemic illness - vet visit within 24h
Possible anaemia or internal bleeding - urgent vet
Possible cardiac or respiratory emergency - immediate vet

symptoms.description: Reduced or absent interest in food. Different from being a 'picky eater' - this is a change from normal eating habits.

possible_causes.brief: Broken tooth, gum disease, oral tumour
New home, new pet, owner absence
Mild reaction in first 24-48h after jabs
Kidney, liver, cancer - often the first sign

home_care: Offer a small amount of bland, appealing food (warm chicken, plain rice)
Try slightly warming food to enhance smell
Hand-feed small pieces if needed
Ensure water intake continues
Check mouth gently for broken teeth, growths, or smell
Remove competing pets at meal times in case of bullying

vet_urgency.low: Skipped one meal but otherwise normal - monitor for 24 hours
vet_urgency.medium: Not eating for 24-48 hours, especially with other mild symptoms - phone vet
vet_urgency.high: Not eating for more than 48 hours, or sudden complete refusal with other symptoms - vet visit

red_flags: Not eating for more than 48 hours
Combined with vomiting, diarrhoea, or lethargy
Drooling excessively or pawing at mouth
Puppy not eating for more than 12 hours (hypoglycaemia risk)

combinations.concern: Chronic illness investigation needed
Classic signs of diabetes, kidney disease - vet visit
Systemic illness - vet within 24h

symptoms.description: Favouring one or more limbs, reluctance to bear weight, or visibly abnormal gait.

possible_causes.brief: Sprain, muscle pull from rough play
Often very painful but heals well
Especially after twisting injury - common in larger dogs

home_care: Restrict exercise immediately - lead walks only for toilet, no running or jumping
Examine paw pads, between toes, and nails for cuts, thorns, or stones
Check for swelling, heat, or sensitivity along the leg (gently)
Allow rest on a soft, supportive surface
Cold compress for fresh injury (10 minutes, wrapped in towel)
Do not give human painkillers - paracetamol and ibuprofen are toxic to dogs

vet_urgency.low: Mild limp after exercise, improving with rest within 24-48h - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent limp for more than 48 hours, or moderate pain - phone vet
vet_urgency.high: Cannot bear any weight, obvious fracture, severe pain, swelling, or combined with collapse - emergency vet

red_flags: Visible deformity or bone protrusion (open fracture)
Limping after road traffic accident or fall - check for internal injuries

combinations.concern: Possible fracture, infection, or joint disease - vet visit
Possible tick-borne disease or systemic infection - vet visit
Possible polyarthritis or immune disease - vet investigation

symptoms.description: Frequent or intense scratching, biting, licking, or rubbing of the skin.

possible_causes.brief: Most common cause; check for flea dirt at base of tail
Often beef, chicken, dairy, or wheat

home_care: Check thoroughly for fleas - run a flea comb over base of tail and belly
Apply vet-recommended flea treatment (Frontline, Advocate, etc.)
Bathe with a gentle, soap-free dog shampoo (oatmeal-based)
Use an Elizabethan collar if licking is causing self-trauma
Avoid known triggers (certain grasses, foods)
Add Omega-3 fish oil supplement (vet-approved) for skin health

vet_urgency.low: Occasional scratching with no skin damage - monitor and treat for fleas
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent itching for more than 1 week or visible skin damage - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Severe self-trauma, open wounds, bleeding, infected skin, or signs of distress - urgent vet

red_flags: Open wounds, bleeding, or hot spots
Pus, foul odour, or yellowish crusts
Combined with lethargy or loss of appetite
Multiple pets in household showing same signs (possible mange)

combinations.concern: Possible mange, allergy, or hormonal disease - vet visit
Likely skin infection - vet for antibiotics/antifungals
Likely ear infection or mites - vet visit

symptoms.description: Forceful expulsion of air from the lungs, ranging from soft cough to loud honking sound.
home_care: Keep environment calm, cool, and dust-free
Use a harness instead of collar to reduce tracheal pressure
Avoid exposure to other dogs while coughing (contagious risk)
Humidifier or steamy bathroom can ease irritation
A teaspoon of honey may soothe a mild cough (adults only, not puppies)
Ensure vaccinations are up to date - kennel cough vaccine available

vet_urgency.low: Occasional, mild cough; dog otherwise bright and eating - monitor 24-48h
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent cough lasting more than a few days, or worsening - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Difficulty breathing, blue/grey gums, collapsing, coughing up blood - emergency vet

red_flags: Blue, purple, or grey tongue or gums
Difficulty breathing or open-mouth breathing at rest
Coughing up blood or pink froth

combinations.concern: Possible pneumonia - vet within 24h
Heart failure or severe respiratory - emergency vet
Possible heart disease or lungworm - vet investigation

symptoms.description: Significantly increased water intake. Most healthy dogs drink roughly 50-100ml per kg of body weight per day.

possible_causes.brief: More water needed with dry kibble
Pot belly, hair loss, increased appetite

home_care: Monitor water intake by measuring bowl
Note any changes in urination (frequency, volume, accidents)
Check weight regularly
Don't restrict water - can be dangerous; restrict only on vet's instruction
Note other changes: appetite, energy, coat condition
Take note of urine colour - very pale or very dark are both concerning

vet_urgency.low: Slightly increased after exercise/heat, resolves - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent increase over several days with no clear cause - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Drinking and urinating excessively with weight loss, lethargy, vomiting - vet within 24h; intact female with vaginal discharge - emergency

red_flags: Vaginal discharge in unspayed female (pyometra emergency)

combinations.concern: Classic diabetes signs - vet for blood test
Possible kidney disease - vet investigation
Could be diabetic ketoacidosis - urgent vet

symptoms.description: Discharge from one or both eyes, ranging from clear watering to thick yellow/green pus.

possible_causes.brief: Pollen, dust, smoke; usually clear discharge
Thick sticky discharge, reduced tear production
Visible red lump in corner of eye

home_care: Gently clean discharge with cooled boiled water and clean gauze
Use a separate piece of cotton for each eye
Wipe from inner corner outward
Prevent dog from rubbing eyes (Elizabethan collar if needed)
Check for foreign material (gently)
Do not use human eye drops without veterinary advice

vet_urgency.low: Small amount of clear discharge, dog comfortable - clean and monitor 24h
vet_urgency.medium: Yellow/green discharge or persistent for more than 24-48h - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Severe pain, eye closed shut, cloudy or bulging eye, sudden vision loss - emergency vet

red_flags: Cloudy or bluish appearance to the eye

combinations.concern: Possible upper respiratory infection - vet
Possible corneal ulcer or foreign body - vet within 24h
Possible glaucoma - emergency vet, can cause permanent blindness in hours

symptoms.description: Repeated head shaking, scratching at ears, ear odour, or visible redness/discharge inside the ear.

possible_causes.brief: Blood-filled swelling of ear flap from shaking

home_care: Examine ear gently for redness, debris, foreign material
Smell ear - yeasty or foul odour suggests infection
Clean only the visible outer ear with vet-approved cleaner
Never insert cotton buds (Q-tips) deep into the ear
Keep ears dry after swimming or bathing
Treat any fleas (which can carry ear mites)

vet_urgency.low: Occasional head shake, no signs of pain or smell - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent shaking/scratching, discharge, smell - vet within 1-2 days
vet_urgency.high: Severe pain, head tilt, balance problems, swollen ear flap - vet within 24h

red_flags: Vomiting along with ear signs (possible middle ear)

combinations.concern: Possible middle/inner ear infection - vet within 24h
Possible allergies - vet investigation
Aural haematoma - vet visit to drain

symptoms.description: Foul-smelling breath that is unusual or worse than normal 'dog breath'.

possible_causes.brief: Most common cause - over 80% of dogs over 3 years
Often a facial swelling under eye
Sweet or fruity smell can indicate ketoacidosis

home_care: Brush teeth daily with dog-specific toothpaste (never human toothpaste)
Provide dental chews (VOHC-approved are best)
Use dental water additives or oral rinses (vet-approved)
Feed appropriate dental diet if vet recommends
Check mouth gently for broken teeth, growths, or red gums
Schedule professional dental cleaning if recommended

vet_urgency.low: Mild doggy breath, no other symptoms - improve dental routine, mention at next check-up
vet_urgency.medium: Foul breath, visible tartar or red gums - vet appointment for dental check
vet_urgency.high: Severe foul odour with reduced eating, facial swelling, drooling, or systemic illness signs - vet within days

red_flags: Facial swelling, especially below the eye
Breath with ammonia/urine smell (possible kidney disease)
Sweet/fruity breath with increased drinking (possible diabetic ketoacidosis)

combinations.concern: Possible dental pain or oral disease - vet visit
Possible kidney disease or diabetes - vet for bloods
Likely tooth root abscess - vet within days

symptoms.description: Any new growth, swelling, or mass under or on the skin.

possible_causes.brief: Soft, moveable, painless - common in older dogs
Often resembles a small bump or wart
Especially in young dogs or seniors
Can vary in size; needs biopsy
Enlarged lymph nodes under jaw, shoulders, groin
Especially around belly button or groin

home_care: Measure and photograph the lump for tracking
Note location, size, texture (soft/hard), mobility, and any pain
Check for additional lumps
Don't squeeze, pop, or irritate the lump
Monitor for rapid growth, colour changes, or discharge
Avoid letting the dog lick or chew the area (cone if needed)

vet_urgency.low: Small, soft, slow-growing lump in older dog - mention at next routine check
vet_urgency.medium: Any new lump - vet appointment for examination (and likely fine needle aspirate)
vet_urgency.high: Rapidly growing, ulcerated, bleeding, painful lump or sudden facial swelling with breathing issues - urgent vet

red_flags: Rapid growth over days or weeks
Combined with weight loss or lethargy
Sudden facial swelling with breathing difficulty (anaphylaxis)
Lumps in lymph node areas (jaw, shoulders, groin)

combinations.concern: Possible malignancy - vet investigation
Possible lymphoma or systemic disease - vet visit
Possible anaphylaxis - emergency vet

symptoms.description: Unexplained or unintentional reduction in body weight over weeks to months.

possible_causes.brief: Especially in middle-aged to older dogs

home_care: Weigh dog weekly on same scales for accuracy
Track food intake - is appetite increased, decreased, or normal?
Note water consumption
Check for parasites with stool sample
Ensure deworming is up to date
Photograph dog from above and side weekly for body condition tracking

vet_urgency.low: Small weight loss with clear cause (recent diet change, increased exercise) - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Unexplained loss of 5-10% body weight over weeks - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Severe loss with lethargy, vomiting, or other symptoms - vet within 24h

red_flags: Loss of more than 10% body weight over weeks
Combined with increased thirst and urination

combinations.concern: Likely diabetes - vet bloodwork
Likely kidney disease - vet bloodwork
Possible IBD, parasites, or malabsorption - vet investigation

symptoms.description: Increase in body weight beyond ideal range, or sudden abdominal enlargement.

possible_causes.brief: Most common cause; over 50% of UK pets overweight

home_care: Use a measuring cup for accurate food portions
Reduce treats; substitute with vegetables (carrot, green beans)
Increase gentle exercise gradually (vet check first)
Weigh weekly and track progress
Use slow-feeder bowls and puzzle feeders
Avoid table scraps and high-calorie chews

vet_urgency.low: Gradual gain with overfeeding - implement diet plan, weigh monthly
vet_urgency.medium: Significant gain despite calorie restriction - vet appointment for blood tests
vet_urgency.high: Sudden abdominal swelling, especially with breathing difficulty or lethargy - emergency vet

red_flags: Sudden abdominal swelling (possible bloat or fluid)
Pot belly with thin legs (possible Cushing's)
Combined with hair loss and lethargy (possible hypothyroidism)

combinations.concern: Likely hypothyroidism - vet bloodwork
Possible Cushing's disease - vet bloodwork
Possible GDV (bloat) - emergency

symptoms.description: Visible effort breathing, including rapid breathing at rest, open-mouth breathing, exaggerated chest/abdominal movement, or stretched neck.

possible_causes.brief: Fluid or air in chest cavity

home_care: Move to a cool, calm, quiet environment immediately
Avoid stress and over-handling
Offer water if dog can drink safely
Do not give human medications
Check gum colour - pale, blue, or grey is emergency
Count breaths per minute (normal 10-30 at rest)

vet_urgency.low: Brief panting after exercise that resolves - normal
vet_urgency.medium: Increased breathing rate at rest with no clear cause - same-day vet
vet_urgency.high: Open-mouth breathing at rest, blue/grey gums, collapse, choking, or breathing with neck stretched - EMERGENCY vet immediately

red_flags: Blue, purple, or grey gums and tongue (cyanosis)
Open-mouth breathing at rest (especially serious in cats but also dogs)
Breathing with neck stretched out and elbows away from body
Choking and unable to make noise

combinations.concern: Likely heart disease - urgent vet
Anaphylaxis - emergency vet immediately
Heatstroke - cool slowly, emergency vet

symptoms.description: Urinating more often than usual or in larger volumes, or having accidents in the house in a previously house-trained dog.

possible_causes.brief: With increased thirst and weight loss

home_care: Track water intake and urination frequency
Note urine colour and any odour
Take more frequent toilet breaks
Provide easier outdoor access for older dogs
Do not punish for accidents - usually a medical issue
Collect a fresh urine sample if vet visit planned

vet_urgency.low: Increased frequency after extra drinking or hot day - monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Persistent increase or new accidents in house - vet appointment for urine test
vet_urgency.high: Straining to urinate with no output (emergency in males), blood in urine, severe lethargy - vet within 24h

red_flags: Straining to urinate with little or no output (blockage)
Combined with vomiting or severe lethargy

combinations.concern: Likely diabetes - vet bloodwork
Possible bladder stones or UTI - vet within 24h
Urinary blockage - emergency vet (especially males)

symptoms.description: Urine appears red, pink, brown, or contains visible blood. Always abnormal.
home_care: Collect a fresh urine sample if possible (clean dish slid under)
Note frequency, amount, and any straining
Provide plenty of fresh water
Don't restrict water intake
Check for other bleeding (gums, nose, stool) - possible clotting issue
Note any recent toxin exposure (rat bait, blue antifreeze)

vet_urgency.low: Female in season (vaginal blood not UTI) - monitor, normal
vet_urgency.medium: Any actual urinary blood without other symptoms - vet within 24-48h
vet_urgency.high: Blood with straining, no urine output, vomiting, collapse, or suspected poisoning - emergency vet

red_flags: Other bleeding (nose, gums, bruises - possible poisoning)

combinations.concern: Likely UTI or stones - vet within 24h
Possible clotting disorder or poisoning - emergency vet
Possible kidney or urinary obstruction - urgent vet

symptoms.description: Difficulty passing stool, straining without success, or absence of bowel movements for more than 48 hours.

possible_causes.brief: Impacted glands cause pain when pooing

home_care: Increase water intake (add water to food)
Add a tablespoon of plain pumpkin purée (not pie filling) to meals
Brief gentle exercise to stimulate bowel
Check anal area for matted fur or pain
Don't give human laxatives without vet advice
Monitor for any output and condition of stool

vet_urgency.low: One missed bowel movement, dog otherwise well - monitor 24-48h
vet_urgency.medium: No movement for 48+ hours or persistent straining - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Straining with vomiting, pain, bloated abdomen, or suspected foreign body - urgent vet

red_flags: Straining with no result and severe distress

combinations.concern: Possible obstruction - emergency vet
May actually be urinary blockage - check
Possible anal gland issue - vet visit

symptoms.description: Patchy or generalised loss of fur, or coat becoming noticeably thinner.

possible_causes.brief: Circular bald patches; contagious to humans
Symmetrical hair loss on flanks; usually no itching
Thin skin, pot belly, increased thirst

home_care: Check for fleas with flea comb
Apply flea treatment (vet-approved)
Photograph bald areas to track progression
Note if itchy or not (key diagnostic clue)
Bathe with mild hypoallergenic shampoo if irritated
Keep environment clean and reduce stress

vet_urgency.low: Mild seasonal shedding - normal
vet_urgency.medium: Patches of hair loss persistent over weeks - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Rapid widespread loss with skin damage or systemic illness signs - urgent vet

red_flags: Combined with severe itching and skin damage
Combined with weight changes and lethargy
Symmetric pattern on both sides of body (hormonal)

combinations.concern: Likely hypothyroidism - vet bloodwork
Possible Cushing's - vet bloodwork
Likely allergy or parasitic - vet visit

symptoms.description: A red, moist, painful patch of irritated skin, often caused by intense licking or scratching of one area.

possible_causes.brief: Single flea can trigger in allergic dogs

home_care: Carefully clip fur around the hot spot to expose air
Clean gently with cooled boiled water or saline
Apply Elizabethan collar to prevent further licking
Avoid creams not specifically vet-approved
Treat fleas if present
Keep area dry and clean

vet_urgency.low: Tiny, dry, healing area - clean and monitor
vet_urgency.medium: Spreading, weeping, painful spot - vet appointment within 1-2 days
vet_urgency.high: Large infected area with pus, fever, or systemic signs - urgent vet

red_flags: Dog cannot stop licking despite cone
Recurrent hot spots in same location

combinations.concern: Flea allergy dermatitis - need rigorous flea control
Anal gland issue - vet to express
Allergy diagnosis needed - vet visit

symptoms.description: Gums appear paler than normal (should be salmon pink). May be white, grey, blue, or yellow. ALWAYS a concerning sign.

possible_causes.brief: Reduced red blood cells from various causes
From blood loss, dehydration, or sepsis

home_care: Pale or coloured gums are an EMERGENCY - go to vet immediately
Note any other symptoms (lethargy, breathing, recent injury)
Check capillary refill: press gum, should return to pink within 2 seconds
Keep dog warm and calm during transport
Do not give food or water if collapse possible
Bring any suspected toxins to vet

vet_urgency.low: (no low category - this is always urgent)
vet_urgency.medium: Slightly pale but dog stable - same-day vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: White, blue, grey, or yellow gums - EMERGENCY vet immediately

red_flags: Any gum discolouration is a red flag in itself
Combined with collapse, weakness, or breathing difficulty
Capillary refill time longer than 2 seconds

combinations.concern: Possible shock or severe bleeding - emergency
Possible internal bleeding (e.g. ruptured spleen) - emergency
Cardiac or respiratory emergency

symptoms.description: Involuntary muscle activity ranging from twitching to full collapse with loss of consciousness, paddling, drooling, and loss of bladder/bowel control.

possible_causes.brief: Most common cause; onset typically 6 months to 6 years
Especially new seizures in older dogs
Especially in puppies and toy breeds

home_care: Stay calm; do NOT put hands near mouth - dog will not swallow tongue
Move furniture or hazards away to prevent injury
Time the seizure - over 5 minutes is emergency
Dim lights and keep environment quiet
Cool the dog if hot (a seizure raises body temperature)
Note triggers, time, and what happened
Allow recovery time in safe space afterwards

vet_urgency.low: (no low category - all seizures need vet assessment)
vet_urgency.medium: First-ever seizure that lasted less than 2 minutes, dog now recovered - vet within 24h
vet_urgency.high: Seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, multiple seizures in 24 hours, or seizure followed by other illness signs - EMERGENCY vet immediately

red_flags: Single seizure lasting more than 5 minutes (status epilepticus)
Two or more seizures in 24 hours (cluster seizures)
First seizure in dog over 5 years old (rule out brain tumour)
Combined with vomiting, fever, or unusual behaviour

combinations.concern: Toxicity - emergency vet with toxin info
Severe brain event - emergency vet
Possible encephalitis - emergency vet

symptoms.description: Heavy, rapid open-mouth breathing beyond what would be expected from heat or exercise.

possible_causes.brief: Anywhere in the body; dogs pant when in pain

home_care: Move to cool, shaded area
Offer cool (not icy) water
Apply damp towel to belly and paws
Use a fan if available
Note ambient temperature and time of onset
Check gum colour (pink and moist normal)

vet_urgency.low: After exercise or in warm weather, resolves with rest - normal
vet_urgency.medium: Panting at rest, in cool environment, lasting more than an hour - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Severe panting with collapse, vomiting, or in extreme heat - EMERGENCY (likely heatstroke)

red_flags: Panting with limping or pain signs

combinations.concern: Heatstroke - cool slowly, emergency vet
Heart disease - vet within 24h
Cushing's - vet bloodwork

symptoms.description: Unusual behaviour including circling, head pressing against walls, getting stuck in corners, staring blankly, or appearing confused.

possible_causes.brief: Sudden head tilt, circling, nausea; often improves
Especially small breeds, gets worse after meals

home_care: Confine to small, safe area to prevent injury
Remove hazards (stairs, sharp edges)
Speak calmly and reassure
Do not force food/water if very disoriented
Note all symptoms and timing
Do not let outside unsupervised

vet_urgency.low: (no low category for new disorientation)
vet_urgency.medium: Mild confusion in senior dog, possible cognitive dysfunction - vet appointment
vet_urgency.high: Sudden disorientation, head tilt, circling, head pressing, or after possible toxin exposure - urgent vet

red_flags: Head pressing against walls (often serious brain disease)

combinations.concern: Vestibular disease - vet within 24h
Serious brain event - emergency vet
Liver disease, brain tumour, or toxicity - urgent vet

symptoms.description: Sudden loss of strength causing the dog to fall down, which may or may not include loss of consciousness.
home_care: Sudden collapse is ALWAYS an emergency - call vet immediately while preparing to transport
Keep dog calm, warm, and on their side if possible
Note time of collapse, surroundings, recent activity
Check gum colour and breathing
Do not offer food or water
Note any recent toxin exposure or food
Transport to vet as fast as safely possible

vet_urgency.low: (no low category - all collapse is emergency)
vet_urgency.medium: Brief faint with rapid full recovery (less than 30 seconds) - vet within 24h
vet_urgency.high: Any collapse with prolonged unconsciousness, pale gums, breathing changes, or in summer heat - EMERGENCY immediately

red_flags: Loss of consciousness for more than 30 seconds

combinations.concern: Possible internal bleeding (e.g. splenic mass rupture) - emergency
GDV - emergency vet immediately
Anaphylaxis - emergency vet
Heatstroke - cool slowly, emergency vet
